majorEmployers.name: Jefferson County Hospital
majorEmployers.notes: 18-bed Critical Access Hospital operated by Duncan Regional Hospital. Opened in 1972. Provides 24/7 emergency services, skilled nursing, rehabilitation and imaging services.

majorEmployers.name: Waurika Public Schools
majorEmployers.notes: Public school district serving approximately 424-450 students in grades PK-12 across 3 schools. Major employer in Waurika.

majorEmployers.name: Jefferson County Government
majorEmployers.notes: County government offices including courthouse, county clerk, court clerk, assessor, treasurer, and sheriff's department.

majorEmployers.name: Chickasaw Nation
majorEmployers.notes: Approximately two-thirds of Jefferson County lies within the Chickasaw Nation jurisdiction. The tribe provides various governmental services and economic development programs in the county.

uniqueFacts: The town of Ringling was named after John Ringling of the famous Ringling Brothers Circus, who founded the town in 1914 after purchasing land surrounding the western terminus of the Ringling Railroad.
The 98th Meridian, which served as the dividing line between Indian Territory and Oklahoma Territory, runs directly through Waurika. A historical marker on State Highway 70 designates this boundary.

The San Bernardo (Taovayas Village) Site, located along the Red River in Jefferson County, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. In 1759, Spanish forces attacked the Wichita Twin Villages here in the largest 18th-century military engagement in present-day Oklahoma.

The Chisholm Trail, one of the most famous cattle trails in American history, passed through Jefferson County. From 1867 to 1887, over four million cattle were driven along this route. U.S. Highway 81 now roughly follows the old trail path.

Waurika Lake, a 10,100-acre reservoir, was created in 1971 by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers by damming Beaver Creek. The lake extends into northwestern Jefferson County and provides fishing, camping, and water recreation.
The Rock Island Passenger Depot in Waurika, built in 1912 in Tudor Revival style, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places and now serves as a public library and museum.
processServingNotes: Jefferson County is in the 5th Judicial District of Oklahoma. The District Court is located on the 3rd floor of the courthouse at 220 N. Main St. in Waurika. Process servers should check in with the Court Clerk's office at (580) 228-2961.

Approximately two-thirds of Jefferson County lies within the Chickasaw Nation tribal jurisdiction. Process servers should verify whether service involves tribal lands or tribal members, as this may affect jurisdiction and service requirements.

The county has a small population spread across 774 square miles, with many residents in rural areas. Process servers should plan for travel time between addresses, particularly when serving papers in outlying communities like Ringling, Ryan, and Terral.
heroLead: Jefferson County sits in southern Oklahoma along the Red River, where the historic Chisholm Trail once drove millions of Texas longhorns northward. From the circus-founded town of Ringling to the Taovayas Village battlefield on the National Register of Historic Places, this county blends ranching heritage with fascinating history across 774 square miles. For process serving in Jefferson County, call Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832 or visit /contact.

faqs.question: Where is the Jefferson County Courthouse located?
faqs.answer: The Jefferson County Courthouse is located at 220 N. Main St., Waurika, OK 73573. The District Court Clerk's office is on the 3rd Floor. The courthouse is open Monday through Friday from 8:00 AM to 4:00 PM. The court clerk can be reached at (580) 228-2961. Jefferson County is part of Oklahoma's 5th Judicial District.

faqs.question: What towns are in Jefferson County where process serving may be needed?
faqs.answer: Jefferson County has eight incorporated towns: Waurika (the county seat), Ringling, Ryan, Terral, Cornish, Hastings, Addington, and Sugden. The largest population center is Waurika with approximately 1,800 residents. For process serving anywhere in Jefferson County, including the smaller rural communities, contact Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832.

faqs.question: Is Jefferson County part of any tribal jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Yes, approximately two-thirds of Jefferson County lies within the Chickasaw Nation's jurisdictional territory. The Chickasaw Nation's Pickens Legislative District includes Jefferson County along with parts of Carter and Love counties. When serving process in Jefferson County, it is important to determine whether the matter involves tribal jurisdiction, particularly for service on tribal members or within Chickasaw Nation boundaries. For assistance with process serving in Jefferson County, call Just Legal Solutions.

faqs.question: When was Jefferson County founded and who was it named after?
faqs.answer: Jefferson County was created in 1907 when Oklahoma achieved statehood. It was named after Thomas Jefferson, the third President of the United States and principal author of the Declaration of Independence. The county was formed from a portion of Comanche County in Oklahoma Territory and the southwestern corner of the Chickasaw Nation. The 98th Meridian, which divided Indian Territory from Oklahoma Territory, runs through Waurika. For process serving help in Jefferson County, call (539) 367-6832.

faqs.question: What is the population and geography of Jefferson County?
faqs.answer: Jefferson County has an estimated population of approximately 5,374 residents as of 2024, making it the 69th most populous of Oklahoma's 77 counties. The county covers 774 square miles, of which 759 are land and 15 are water. The county is located in southern Oklahoma along the Red River, bordered by Stephens County to the north, Carter County to the northeast, Love County to the east, Cotton County to the west, and Texas counties to the south. The largest city is Waurika with about 1,800 residents. Call (539) 367-6832 for process serving throughout Jefferson County.

faqs.question: What are some unique historical facts about Jefferson County?
faqs.answer: Jefferson County has a rich history. The town of Ringling was founded by circus magnate John Ringling of Ringling Brothers fame in 1914. The famous Chisholm Trail cattle route passed through the county, with millions of longhorns driven north between 1867 and 1887. The San Bernardo (Taovayas Village) Site along the Red River marks the location of a 1759 battle between Spanish forces and Wichita Indians. The historic Rock Island Railroad Depot, built in 1912 in Waurika, is now a museum listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Waurika Lake provides 10,100 acres of recreation. For process serving in Jefferson County, contact Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832.
